text:
A few showers this morning with overcast skies during the afternoon hours. High 59F. Winds WNW at 10 to 15 mph. Chance of rain 40%..

Partly cloudy skies. Low 42F. Winds NNW at 5 to 10 mph.

Updated: December 2, 2025 @ 11:46 am





Jonah Chester covers flooding, sea level rise and climate change
for the Post and Courier's Rising Waters Lab.

Jonah Chester covers flooding, sea level rise and climate change
for the Post and Courier's Rising Waters Lab.

France has spent around $1.5 billion to address fecal contamination in the Seine River, one of the 2024 Olympic venues for aquatic sports. High E. Coli levels in the river have persisted despite those efforts, forcing the temporary postponement of the men's triathlon and thecancellation of training in the river.

Charleston is in no position to judge, though. The city has its share of poopy water bodies.

And they wouldn't pass for an Olympic venue.

"I took a look at our data for the past couple of weeks to see where we might have been able to host the Olympic open-water swimming and triathlon races," Andrew Wunderley, executive director of Charleston Waterkeeper, said. "We wouldn't have been able to host in Shem Creek, James Island Creek, Filbin Creek or in the Ashley River."

Waterkeeper conductswidespread pollution testingof Charleston-area waterbodies, and issuesswimming advisoriesfor area creeks, rivers and beaches.

Throughout July, the highest levels of enterococcus (a fecal bacteria) pollution documented by Waterkeeper at the four Charleston-area waterbodies were:

"That's actually the upper limit of the test," Wunderley said about Filbin Creek. "We know the number was at least 24,000, but it could have been higher because we're not able to measure above that limit."

For reference, World Triathlon, the international governing body for the sport, sets themaximum recommended enterococci pollutionat 185 CFU/100 mL for "sea and transitional waters." The agency considers anything less than 100 CFU/100 mL an "excellent" amount of poop.

South Carolina'sstate swimming standardis 104 CFU/100 mL. Anything in excess of that level causes state regulators to issue a public notice.

The Associated Press reported Olympic officials postponed the July 30 men's triathlon when E. Coli levels in the Seine spiked above900CFU/100 mL for three testing sites.

(That's the maximum recommended E. Coli level for inland waters set by World Triathlon, but AP reports it's not a "magic number" and officials could factor in other elements when making the call on whether to proceed with events.)

"While swimming under the bridge, I felt and saw things that we shouldn't think about too much," Belgian triathlete Jolien Vermeylen tolda United Kingdom-based news outletabout her experience in the Seine.

Because of Charleston's brackish water, Waterkeeper tests for enterococcus bacteria. French officials are also testing the Seine for enterococcus, but their decision on whether to proceed is largely based on E. Coli levels, according to the AP.

Wunderley said E. Coli is the standard indicator of fecal contamination in fresh water, while enterococcus is the better indicator for salty and brackish water. Both are also accepted as water-quality indicators by World Triathlon, the international governing body for the sport.

E. Coli levels along the Seine River havefluctuated widelyover the last several weeks. Heavy rainfall can cause levels to spike, and bright and warm weather can cause it to drop.

"For E. coli, on July 21, they were up to 2,000 (colony-forming units) at one site, and around 1,000 at the others," Wunderley said. "They've had some really high bacteria results, but not nearly as high as some of the ones we see here in Charleston."

Just like in France, fecal contamination levels in Charleston's waters can be significantly influenced by weather. Concentrations dipped to as low as 98 CFU/100 mL in Northbridge Park in mid-July, for example.

Mike Marcell, left, and Andrew Wunderley ride along the James Island Creek on Wednesday, July 15, 2020.

The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency strongly advises against swimming through feces-contaminated water, as do most people with common sense. In a 2004 report to Congress, the EPAestimatedthat up to 3.5 million Americans each year fall ill from coming into contact with water contaminated by sewage overflows.

The most common side-effect from swimming in such contaminated water is gastroenteritis, according to the agency.Gastroenteritiscomes with a fun grab-bag of side effects — including, but not limited to, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, headaches and fever. That's on the less-severe end of symptoms, Wunderley said.

"On the other end of the scale, it could be really serious pathogens, like tuberculosis, cholera and Legionnaires Disease," he explained.

Feces in Charleston's waters comes from a variety of sources. Leaky septic tanks, which are loosely regulated in South Carolina, are a major source of waste. Charleston's sewers are another major source.

A sewage overflow is pictured at Oakdale Place in West Ashley.

Paris' fecal woes are tied to the city's centuries-old combined sewage system. Modern sewage systems are split up into stormwater and sanitary lines to help prevent cross-contamination and water pollution.

Although, as can be seen in Charleston's streets after heavy rain, overflows from sanitary sewers can still occur when the system becomes overburdened by stormwater intrusion and ejects wastewater back out of manholes. Those blowbacks could become more severe and frequent as climate change powers stronger tropical storms and more flooding.

"We're getting squeezed here in Charleston between rapid and aggressive growth, a changing climate and rising sea levels," Wunderley said. "Our infrastructure is also getting squeezed between those  things. We really need as a community to invest in water and sewer upgrades."

Wunderley and Charleston Waterkeeper, represented by the Southern Environmental Law Center, earlier this yearthreatened to sueCharleston Water System, alleging that the utility was failing to adequately address chronic sanitary sewage overflows.

In response to the allegations, CWS spokesperson Mike Saia in February said that "overflows are never an acceptable outcome."

"Our current $123 million wastewater capital program consists of projects that directly address all areas prone to sewer overflows," he wrote in an email at the time. "Since 2015, we’ve spent $220 million on wastewater capital improvement projects addressing system capacity and reliability, which help to reduce the potential for overflows."

Wunderley said any potential litigation is on hold, as CWS and Waterkeeper are "still in talks" on the best path forward to address the issue.

Follow Jonah Chester on Twitter @chester_jonah.

title: Rivers, creeks in Charleston face severe fecal contamination
author: Jonah Chester jchester@postandcourier.com
description: Fecal pollution in the Seine is an issue during the 2024 Olympics. In Charleston, Shem Creek, Ashley River and others are also contaminated with poop bacteria.
